sentences.text: Absolutely.
Typhus, lice, rashes.
But everything was healed.
Some medication was made by itself, you know, in the old systems from the-- from the farmers, from-- from the old people, that, you know, from the villages.
It-- we helped as much as we could.
Nobody died.
There was not one case of death from all those.
They were weak, they had special attention.
The hospital always got more milk, more other stuff to feed them, to bring them to themselves.
But it wasn't easy.
I, personally, did not have none of those things.
No.
I got sick, you know, with a cold, with this, with that.
Snow-- you lived in snow.
I have plenty of arthritis from laying on the wet snow, or on the wet grass or, you know.
It wasn't a picnic, that's for sure.
But nothing compared to concentration camps.
Nothing.
Not even close.
Tovia was sick quite a few times.
Tovia was sick one time with very high temperature, which it was probably an infection in his body.
But he was very well taken care.
The doctors.
Dr.
Hersch was there.
And we had-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we had about three doctors.
Tamara was there.
I don't remember even her second name.
And then there was another doctor, a gynecologist, that took care of a lot of-- there was quite a few abortions.
There were quite a few births.
There were quite a few miscarriages.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Tovia was shot one time.
I don't know if it was on purpose or not.
But he died with the-- with the mark on his neck, because not a German, but a-- a Russian guy-- I don't know if he specially did it or not, but a--
A bullet went through here, and left-- and passed by, and left him in a big opening.
It healed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had certain operations in our-- done in these circumstances.
They operated on a tumor of my brother-in-law, the youngest.
This was called other doctors from different brigades, that they were very known as surgeons from before the war.
And they were accidentally-- they were from Lida, but they were accidentally in different brigades.
Dr.
Messing, who just died two years ago.
And he operated-- Dr.
Levin operated on my brother-in-law.
Dr.
Pupko operated.
They were called from other-- they gladly came to help.
I told you, the-- the biggest antibiotic.
When we came out from the woods, we found out about penicillin.
But until then, we didn't know nothing about it.
And if we would know-- lately, already, from Russia, when the planes used to stop on the-- by the general platoon-- he had a special base where there were planes.
It's like a-- you know, like an airport.
And each one had to supply and support us.
A Lot of people, from-- strangers from different brigades, used to come to us.
They used to give us something for repairing their guns, for making their-- a pair of shoes, for sewing them a pair of pants.
They used to trade with us.
We got from them what-- what they asked.
I don't know in details what the-- what the command-- the brigade, you know, the commanders or the one that were in charge of it, asked for.
But I know that it was paid for it.
They had to saddle a horse or something.
We had all that things.
People used to take off, like you're going, now, let's say, to Atlantic City, to-- to the-- to Florida, to the Bahamas.
That's how they came from different brigades to look.
That was their vacation, was to come to us and look at it, and spend, have a good time.
They called us [NON-ENGLISH].
The Russians called it Jerusalem.
But the-- between us, they called it [NON-ENGLISH].
I would say yes.
Like I said before, there were plenty of complaints.
And it was expected, because you cannot satisfy everybody.
One said they had too much to eat.
The other one said they didn't have enough to eat.
The third one said, I-- he had boots.
I had to wait in line for until my boots were repaired.
No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Where could we get birth control?
I think-- well, not too many.
I lost a child in the woods, too.
I lost a child on the way back to Israel.
But this was a bigger child.
A normal child, normal birth.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At home.
No.
We had a special gynecologist.
She has a brother here in Baltimore.
In the woods?
No.
We didn't have such big children.
The oldest probably was about 10 from the young.
And this was still mostly girls than boys,
Yeah.
They sang Hebrew songs.
They sang Zionist songs.
And they sure enough, they had to-- to cover with Russian songs.
Right.
In the woods, there seems to be a rivalry between people that came from different shtetlekh.
Right.
In the woods, we're one thing.
Or you're a Communist.

And we have a lot of problem for the real Communists because a lot of people were sent not Jewish to us, like to be in the-- in the government of the-- of the brigade, in the management of the brigade.
And they tried to take over, and their only complaint is that we're more Zionist than Communist.
And this was against the Russian law.
At that time, communism was-- you know, until Ber-- he came Borgachev-- what's his name?
Gorbachev.
Gorbachev.
You-- I don't have to tell you how the communism was there.
We tried to hide the Zionism when it's needed.
But in everybody's heart, everybody knew in his own heart that we are Zionist, and we are Jews.
And nothing can take us away.
Now, Tovia had-- he was an outstanding speaker.
Was very emotional, but he was a very good speaker.
And when he spoke, the trees shivered.
You had to move.
You had to bring in new rules.
You had to do this.
You were-- rules and regulations, what you are allowed, what you are not allowed.
That we have to move the base.
That we have to go.
That-- you had some-- you had to keep the-- the people's spirit, because they were-- if it came a bad few days, or a raid, or what-- people were very pessimistic.
Because according his spirit.
According his spirit.
They believed in him, I'm telling you.
They always knew, if he's on the base, they'll survive.

If he's not, that he had to go and see somebody, or he had to go to General Platon, or he had to go here, or there, some kind of action, or-- you know, you couldn't tell the whole-- you don't tell the army what the generals are doing, or what they're preparing.
That's strictly confidential.
He knew the rules because of the-- excuse me-- because of the-- because he served in the Army, and he knew.
And he wasn't the plain solder in the army, too.
I remember, he told you, I think, the story when he-- when he fought, already, for his Zionism, being in the army, being 22 years old, that somebody said to him, Jew.
And he smacked him, and then he had a trial and all that.
And he said, I'll protect my-- my belief.
I'll protect who I am.
I'll protect myself, and I'll protect my nation.
And I don't care what kind of a punishment you'll give me.
And he-- and he-- and he didn't have nobody to-- to advise him to do.
But he just protected himself in a-- in a big trial, a military trial.
He put the guy in-- in the hospital for quite a few months, that he cut him-- his face in pieces for just saying Jew, dirty Jew.
That they didn't-- they didn't punish him.
He won the trial.
I serve, he said, for the Polish army.
I'm devoted because I'm here.
But at the same token, I won't let my-- my belief, and my nation, down by-- because I'm a Jew, somebody should threaten me.
It was mostly from Russian news.
Lately.
Lately.
We knew already by Leningrad that it's-- the conditions are very, very bad.
I mean, that they are losing.
Right.
Right.
The Russians-- actually, they saved us.
Because all of a sudden, we saw that the Germans are getting weaker and weaker.
And we heard already that, be careful, I mean, from-- like I said, from the headquarters, that, be careful, because the Germans are getting loose.
They are running.
They are escaping.
They are not organized anymore.
They are not an army anymore.
But with that-- with that, we lost, the last day of our being in the woods, the last day of being in the woods, nine people, that we didn't lose for three years.
Because the Germans.
It was a fight with the Germans.
They still had the-- the-- they still had weapons.
They still had with what to shoot.
And they were running.
They wanted a free-- they wanted a free way of being-- to be able to escape.
And here, the partisans were waiting for them already.
They came on our base, and that's why the fight started.
They killed nine people that day, and next day, we went-- we left the woods and went to [INAUDIBLE].
How did we know?
The Russian army came in.
We heard-- yeah, the Russian army came in, right down the major roads, with the-- with the tanks.
And they sang their song, and they screamed, we came to liberate you.
But be very careful because there is a lot of loose of Ger-- loose Germans.
That they are running, and escaping, and they're not organized.
But they-- they have, still, guns and rifles and all the ammunition, and be very careful.
And that's what happened.
They came on our base.
Plenty of them-- the last days, were plenty [INAUDIBLE], because when they were caught, they-- our people, you know, hurt and discouraged, and thinking, where will they go?
When they'll come even the war's ending, where will they go?
Who will they find?
Who will they see?
Where is their homes?
Where's their children?
Where's their wives?
So-- and the Germans were like-- some of them are like flies.
So they caught them.
They killed them.
They cut pieces from them.
And they were screaming, for my children, for my brother, for my mother.
You know, it was terrible days before the liberation.
They used to bring them on the base and make [INAUDIBLE] for them.
I-- with all my pain, and with all my-- [SIGHS] with all my-- what I went, really, through, I just couldn't see the scene.
I couldn't be at the scene.
They used to cut their fingers.
There was an old man, Pupko.
He lived through the whole war with the whole family from Lida.
He took a piece of wood from the tree, and he was knocking the German, screaming, for my mother, for my father, for my children.
And he wasn't the only one.
There was plenty others.
[INAUDIBLE] We organized.
We got a order from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the headquarters that we can leave the woods.
We organized.
We tried to pack up.
Our suitcases weren't-- were not waiting for us.
Whatever who wants it to take, he took.

And they lined them up, and in order, we went to Navaradok, because that was the closest from us, where it was giving to everyone a-- there was-- they made a big table there, that was already, more or less, a more-- more normal life than we had in the woods.

It was a big table, and the secretary of the-- that worked for Tovia all the time, was sitting and giving out with his orders [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] certificates that they served in the partisans from this and this date, to this and this date.
They are free.
They were liberated this and this date.
I consider the date [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about July 14.
And where did you and Tovia go?
We stayed awhile there because we were very wild.
I never was in Navaradok before.
I never-- my in-laws I never knew from before.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my friends came home, like Raja.
And she had a gun.
I had a gun.
And she knew where her parents-- her parents actually were in Siberia because they transferred them there.
But she didn't go to Siberia.
And she knew where she gave away her stuff before she left for the ghetto, and she knew exactly the housing.
She knew everything.
And, you know, wild people after the woods, having a drink and all that.
We went loose.
We took over the city.
Then, we-- Tovia had plenty of complaints that some crazy people drank too much, and they came to the-- to the-- to the Polish homes and made a lot of mischief.
But the-- the mischief we didn't do.
But, you know, she recognized her-- her clock on the wall.
She recognized-- she recognized the-- the other things from-- from the break fronts.
And she was very upset with that.
So she-- she wanted back and they didn't want to give her, so she threatened them.
So then, they went to the Pol-- to the Russian police, and they said, so, that wild people came here.
We were absolutely wild.
We just came from the woods.
So we were wild.
And they came-- Tovia to scold us.
I thought he would kill us, me and her and all of you.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For us, it was a nice game.
It's, you know, payback.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then, we went to Lida.
We didn't stay in Navaradok too long.
Actually the whole family.
His-- his brother.
Now, he was-- they-- today, a lot of-- a lot of things are denied.
But I don't go with nobody in competition, and I'm not trying to show off.
But I know every step that he did, he was followed by his family.
So I guess he did the right thing, because they would never come out from there if not for him.
He left everything.
You know, a lot of things I picked up, too, from the-- from the Polish guys.
And we settled a home, and he said, he's not going to stay in Russia for no money.
So this was in Lida.
This was in Lida.
He worked, and I worked.
[INAUDIBLE] I worked in a library.
He worked in a different place.
And we weren't happy.
He knew that some day-- because when he went to Minsk to report for the whole years of-- of his being commander in-- from the brigade, and I was alone, and I-- I don't remember.
I wasn't home, anyway.
So my-- my landlord's son was a white Pollack, that was like the Germans.
They worked for the Germans, but they weren't from Germany.
They were from Poland, from the city of Lida and of Grodek, White Pollacks, they were called.
And they worked for the Germans, and they wanted to kill, too.
They liked the work.
So when Tovia wasn't there-- wasn't home, and I was away too, they came to my house and they took, and they looked.
And they looked for me, and they looked for him.
Thank God I wasn't there.
And he wasn't secure there.
He knew that, if not them, some other-- some-- somebody else will kill him there.
Or they'll send him away to Russia, or something will happen.
He wanted to escape.
He had enough.
So after a good year and a half, maybe two-- what year?
We went in '44.
In '45, we left.
Sure.
Sure.
Then we came to Warsaw.
Warsaw was still burning.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Warsaw wasn't liberated yet.
It was liberated, but not officially.
Warsaw was still burning.
One piece of fire.
Tovia, he just closed the door and we took just what we could carry.
And I-- I was pregnant.
And he said to his brothers, he wanted to go.
That's my-- my plan.
You want to go, you're welcome.
Your-- my opinion is that you have to leave here.
Because one brother, Asael, was killed after the war.
He was mobilized to the army, to the Russians, and he was killed.
The day when he was killed, his daughter, Asaela, was born in Israel.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH]
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Not to Warsaw.
We went first to Vilna.
In Vilna, he had two very good connections, my husband, with a Polish guy that was in the woods.
And he hel-- and he was a big shot in Vilna, so he gave him a tremendous hand.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he sent him away.
He didn't let him stay there more than a week.
And I think Tovia met somebody in the street, and he didn't like his approach.
So he wanted to leave, too.
We sent him away, and from Vilna, we went to Bialystok to see if somebody is from my family.
But when I looked at everything, I said, I don't want to sleep over one night here.
Let's go.
We went through to Warsaw.
From Warsaw, we went to Lublin.
Lublin, we stopped.
I think in Bialystok, somebody recognized him, too, Tovia.
And he said that he came for a mission from the place that he works.
They sent him for a-- for some business.
And in Lublin we were 10 days.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And from Lublin, they got connections already with the Shlichim from Israel, and they knew already who Tovia is.
And they told us how [INAUDIBLE], but we never went with the [INAUDIBLE].
We went with legal certificates with a Transylvania boat-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] a boat.
Transylvania.
There was very little.
We were the first ones to leave Lida.
We just closed the door.
I left Persian covers-- Persian rugs that I picked up from the Poles in [INAUDIBLE].
And I left everything.
I just took a small bag in my hand, and I left.
Closed the door, the radio was playing, and I left.
No, we didn't see him.
Nobody.
Nobody.
Nobody.
[INAUDIBLE] We wanted to go to Israel.
We were-- I'm telling you, there were, in Lublin, when we came, it was already Shlichim waiting there.
They were already--
Oh, that was the-- I was Turkish, and I was Spanish, and I was-- and Hebrew was my language.
And all the idiots thought that that's all kind of languages I speak.
And they--
Because that was the direction from-- from-- from Vilna already, how to go.
Somebody gave us a direction how to go.
It wasn't our mind to go.
From Lublin, it was already Shlichim, and they took us over.
They took us to Czechoslovakia.
They took us to Romania.
We stayed a half a year.
And from Romania, we went-- and in Hungary, we were.
And then, the last stop was Romania.
From Romania, we took Transylvania, a boat.
And we went to Israel.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
